description:
A network-engineering audit has revealed that three critical configuration
    backups stored in the directory  
    /home/user/config_backups  
    might have been tampered with.  
    Your job is to verify their SHA-256 checksums against the official reference
    list and to produce a single machine-readable log file that clearly records
    the results of your investigation.

    Files already present on disk:
      • /home/user/config_backups/fw01.conf  
      • /home/user/config_backups/sw02.conf  
      • /home/user/config_backups/rtr03.conf  
      • /home/user/config_backups/reference_checksums.csv  

    1. Read /home/user/config_backups/reference_checksums.csv.  
       It contains the *authoritative* hash values. The file is a simple header-
       plus-data CSV:

           FILENAME,SHA256
           fw01.conf,ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad
           sw02.conf,b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe37a5380ee9088f7ace2efcde9
           rtr03.conf,e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

       (No extra whitespace, exactly one newline at the end.)

    2. Compute the current SHA-256 checksum of each of the three *.conf* files.
       • Launch the checksum calculations *in parallel* (background jobs) so the
         work finishes more quickly, then make sure they have all completed
         before you continue.  
       • You may use whatever checksum utility is available in a standard Linux
         shell (e.g. sha256sum), but do not hard-code the expected values – read
         them from the CSV file.

    3. Generate a verification log named
         /home/user/checksum_verification.log  
       following this *exact* format so that automated grading scripts can parse
       it without ambiguity:

           FILE,EXPECTED_SHA256,ACTUAL_SHA256,STATUS
           fw01.conf,ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad,<calc1>,OK
           sw02.conf,b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe37a5380ee9088f7ace2efcde9,<calc2>,OK
           rtr03.conf,e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855,<calc3>,OK

       • Replace <calc1>, <calc2> and <calc3> with the actual hashes
         you computed.  
       • The STATUS field must be the literal word “OK” when the computed hash
         matches the expected value, otherwise the literal word “MISMATCH”.  
       • Every line, including the header, must be terminated by a single Unix
         newline (LF). No extra blank lines are allowed.  
       • There must be *exactly* four comma-separated columns per line and
         *absolutely no* spaces before or after commas.

    4. When the log is finished, print its contents to STDOUT so a human
       reviewer can see it immediately.

    Acceptance criteria checked by the automatic grader:
      • /home/user/checksum_verification.log exists.  
      • The header row is exactly:  
            FILE,EXPECTED_SHA256,ACTUAL_SHA256,STATUS  
      • There are three data rows, in the same order as in the reference CSV.  
      • The EXPECTED_SHA256 column matches what is listed in
        reference_checksums.csv.  
      • The ACTUAL_SHA256 column contains the correct hashes of the current
        files.  
      • STATUS is “OK” for all three rows (no mismatches).  
      • The file is newline-terminated and contains no superfluous whitespace.
truth:
The testing harness will pre-populate the following files:

    1. /home/user/config_backups/fw01.conf  
       – ASCII text, *no* trailing newline, exact content:  
           abc

    2. /home/user/config_backups/sw02.conf  
       – ASCII text, *no* trailing newline, exact content:  
           hello world

    3. /home/user/config_backups/rtr03.conf  
       – Empty file (size 0 bytes).

    4. /home/user/config_backups/reference_checksums.csv  
       – ASCII text with a single trailing LF, exact content:  
           FILENAME,SHA256
           fw01.conf,ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad
           sw02.conf,b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe37a5380ee9088f7ace2efcde9
           rtr03.conf,e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

    SHA-256 values (authoritative):
      • fw01.conf  → ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad
      • sw02.conf  → b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe37a5380ee9088f7ace2efcde9
      • rtr03.conf → e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

    The grader will verify that /home/user/checksum_verification.log:
      • Exists and is readable by the normal user.
      • Contains the four exact lines (header + three data lines) shown in the
        task description, with the computed hashes identical to the reference
        values and STATUS set to “OK” on each line.
name: task_000000_bc090048
